answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: Health Sector Scenarios | Category | Ambitious and Orderly | Delayed and Disorderly | Hothouse World | IPCC Shared Socio-economic Pathway (SSP) | SSP1-1.9 | SSP2-4.5 | SSP3-7.0
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, Health Sector Scenarios table, p.106
answers.notes: Three health-sector-level scenarios developed in collaboration with Tonkin & Taylor and other healthcare sector entities. Each is mapped to an IPCC SSP.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Ambitious and Orderly: 1.6°C (2041–2060), 1.4°C (2081–2100); Delayed and Disorderly: 2.0°C (2041–2060), 2.7°C (2081–2100); Hothouse World: 2.1°C (2041–2060), 3.6°C (2081–2100). The company's emissions targets are aligned with limiting warming to 1.5°C.
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, Health Sector Scenarios (continued) table, p.107
answers.notes: Temperature outcomes are given for two time windows (2041–2060 and 2081–2100) across all three scenarios. No explicit temperature overshoot language is used.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Tonkin & Taylor (led the Technical Working Group that developed the health sector-level scenarios); IPCC (SSP framework underpinning the scenarios); TCFD and New Zealand External Reporting Board (methodology resources). Oxygen Consulting supported AFT's entity-level scenario analysis process.
answers.evidence: In collaboration with Tonkin & Taylor and other climate reporting entities in the healthcare sector, AFT contributed to the Technical Working Group (TWG) supporting the development of the health sector-level climate change scenarios in 2023 - 2024. The approach to developing the scenarios was based on resources from the Taskforce for Climate Related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) and the New Zealand External Reporting Board.
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, Scenario Analysis – Scenarios section, p.105
answers.notes: Multiple parties are named. Tonkin & Taylor led the sector-level scenario development; IPCC SSPs provide the underlying climate science framework; Oxygen Consulting facilitated AFT's entity-level application.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: IPCC Shared Socio-economic Pathway (SSP) | SSP1-1.9 | SSP2-4.5 | SSP3-7.0
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, Health Sector Scenarios table, p.106
answers.notes: Only SSP identifiers are used; no RCP identifiers appear anywhere in the document. There is no mixing of scenario families.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: AFT elected to use the three scenarios developed at the sector-level as part of its entity-level scenario analysis process, focusing on both the global and New Zealand-specific parameters in line with the scope of its operations, manufacturing, distribution networks and end users.
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, Scenario Analysis – Scenarios section, p.105
answers.notes: The scenarios were developed specifically for the New Zealand healthcare sector through a Technical Working Group process (six-step methodology including mapping drivers to archetypes and drafting narratives), incorporating NZ-specific parameters such as NZ GDP impact, NZ population, and NZ carbon price. This goes beyond using off-the-shelf IPCC SSPs.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: In collaboration with Tonkin & Taylor and other climate reporting entities in the healthcare sector, AFT contributed to the Technical Working Group (TWG) supporting the development of the health sector-level climate change scenarios in 2023 - 2024. Six steps were undertaken, with a series of workshops being held with the TWG as the project progressed.
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, Scenario Analysis – Scenarios section, p.105
answers.notes: The scenarios were developed through a collaborative Technical Working Group (TWG) involving multiple healthcare sector entities and Tonkin & Taylor, with workshops held throughout the process. This constitutes participatory/co-produced scenario development.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.evidence: AFT undertook scenario analysis to identify and assess the scope, size and impact of our climate-related risks and opportunities. The scenario analysis process undertaken in described in the Strategy section. No part of AFT's value chain was excluded from the process.
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, Risk Management section, p.114
answers.notes: The document describes three forward-looking scenarios (Ambitious and Orderly, Delayed and Disorderly, Hothouse World) but does not describe a separate baseline or "business as usual" reference scenario against which the others are compared.

answers.question: Model used
answers.evidence: No modelling was undertaken as part of these processes and the assessment was qualitative in nature only.
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, Risk Management section, p.114
answers.notes: The document explicitly states no modelling was undertaken. No climate models, IAMs, or macroeconomic models are named anywhere in the document.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Extreme weather events may cause interruptions to AFT's product manufacturing and distribution. AFT's business model relies on contract manufacturing, third-party freight, and third-party logistics providers. Extreme weather events such as flooding and storms may cause interruptions to AFT's product manufacturing and distribution, which may be temporary or sustained in nature. This may impact AFT's ability to manufacture products, extend lead times, extend delivery timeframes, and reduce overall access to products for our customers. AFT expects to experience greater business impacts in the event of sustained interruptions that extend beyond 3 weeks.
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, Climate-Related Risks section, p.108
answers.notes: The document discusses potential business disruption from acute physical risks (flooding, storms) but does not provide quantified financial loss/damage estimates. The assessment is qualitative in nature only, as explicitly stated. However, the risk is explicitly included in the scenario analysis.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answer: direct impacts from climate hazards (extreme weather events, wildfires, air quality, infectious diseases, vector-borne diseases)
financial impact of supply chain disruptions
answers.evidence: Carbon price | 2050: NZ$250 tCO2e | 2050: NZ$250 tCO2e | 2050: N/A | NZ GDP impact | Moderate GDP impacts | High GDP impacts | High GDP impacts | NZ population | 2050: 6.13m | 2050: 6.13m | 2050: 6.93m | Percentage NZ >65 years old | 2050: 23.3% | 2050: 23.3% | 2050: 21.4%
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, Health Sector Scenarios (continued) table, p.107; also p.106
answers.notes: Variables are drawn from both the quantitative scenario table (p.107) and the qualitative scenario descriptions (p.106). Sea level rise and extreme weather frequency are mentioned in the time horizons section as physical risks to be managed.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: AFT elected to use the three scenarios developed at the sector-level as part of its entity-level scenario analysis process, focusing on both the global and New Zealand-specific parameters in line with the scope of its operations, manufacturing, distribution networks and end users.
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, Scenario Analysis – Scenarios section, p.105
answers.notes: The scenarios explicitly incorporate both global parameters (e.g., global warming temperatures, global SSP pathways) and New Zealand-specific parameters (NZ GDP impact, NZ population, NZ carbon price, NZ-specific climate hazards).

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short-term: Less than 1 year; Medium-term: Next 1–3 years; Long-term: Next 3–10 years; Very Long-term: Next 11–75 years (spanning to 2100). Rationales are provided for each, aligned with AFT's strategic planning horizons and financial decision-making cycles.
answers.evidence: Time horizon | Alignment with strategic planning horizons and financial decision-making | Short-term | Less than 1 year (aligned with stock planning and budget cycles) | Medium-term | Next 1- 3 years (aligned with supplier and manufacturing management) | Long-term | Next 3 – 10 years (aligned with AFT strategy and product development cycles). | Very Long-term | Next 11 – 75 years (aligned with scenario timeframes). The short, medium, and long term time horizons included in the health sector-level climate span to 2100 to align with projected climate physical impacts over this period. AFT's time horizons align with our strategic planning horizons and financial decision-making.
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, Time Horizons section, p.107
answers.notes: Four time horizons are defined (short, medium, long, very long). Justification is explicitly provided for each, linking them to specific business planning cycles (stock planning, supplier management, strategy/product development, and scenario timeframes respectively). The overall span to 2100 is justified by alignment with projected climate physical impacts.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: These scenarios were chosen due to AFT's involvement in their development and to allow for meaningful comparison of climate statements.
answers.location_hint: Appendix 1: Climate Resilience, Scenario Analysis – Scenarios section, p.105
answers.notes: Two explicit reasons are given: (1) AFT's direct involvement in developing the health sector-level scenarios through the Technical Working Group, and (2) enabling meaningful comparison of climate statements across the healthcare sector. No model justification is provided as no models were used.
